before: How Time Passes is the debut album by trumpeter Don Ellis recorded in 1960 and released on the Candid label. Trumpeter Don Ellis's hard bop sensibility is on display in this 1961 session, a document of the hard bop era.
after: How Time Passes is the debut album by trumpeter Don Ellis recorded in 1960 and released on the Candid label in 1961.
evidenceUsed: wiki lede (recorded 1960, Candid) + MB release date 1961; dropped the contradictory 'this 1961 session' sentence

before: Blythe's second Columbia album reinterprets jazz standards and originals with his signature blend of free jazz intensity and deep tradition. With Fred Hopkins on bass and Steve McCall on drums, Blythe tears through 'In a Sentimental Mood' and Fats Waller's 'Jitterbug Waltz' with fierce, loving energy. Demonstrated that the avant-garde and tradition need not be opposed. Blythe's ability to play 'inside' and 'outside' simultaneously made him one of the most important saxophonists of the late 1970s.
after: Blythe's first Columbia album reinterprets jazz standards and originals with his signature blend of free jazz intensity and deep tradition. With Fred Hopkins on bass and Steve McCall on drums, Blythe tears through 'In a Sentimental Mood' and Fats Waller's 'Jitterbug Waltz' with fierce, loving energy. Demonstrated that the avant-garde and tradition need not be opposed. Blythe's ability to play 'inside' and 'outside' simultaneously made him one of the most important saxophonists of the late 1970s.
evidenceUsed: wiki 'His first album for the Columbia label'; changed only 'second' to 'first', preserved rest verbatim

before: In Their Own Sweet Way is a 1998 studio album by pianist Dave Brubeck and his quintet. Brubeck was accompanied by his four sons on a recording for the first time.
after: In Their Own Sweet Way is a 1997 studio album by pianist Dave Brubeck and his quintet. Brubeck was accompanied by his four sons on a recording for the first time.

before: Recorded in 1957, Inside Hi-Fi showcases Lee Konitz at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. Inside Hi-Fi remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Lee Konitz's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.

before: Recorded in 2010, It Could Happen to You showcases Chet Baker at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. It Could Happen to You remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Chet Baker's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.

It Could Happen to You remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Chet Baker's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
evidenceUsed: MB firstReleaseDate 1958 + wiki '1958 studio album' and 'In 2010, it was remastered and reissued on CD'; corrected the 'Recorded in 2010' claim to a 1958 release with a 2010 reissue

before: Recorded for Pablo in 1992, Kansas City 7 presents pianist Count Basie working in a swing vein. Among the tracks are "Jaylock" and "Exactly Like You". The album comes from the big band era when jazz was America's popular music, driven by dancing and radio.
after: A 1980 Pablo release reissued in 1992, Kansas City 7 presents pianist Count Basie working in a swing vein. Among the tracks are "Jaylock" and "Exactly Like You". The album comes from the big band era when jazz was America's popular music, driven by dancing and radio.
evidenceUsed: wiki '1980 studio album by Count Basie' + MB labels (Pablo 2310-908, Original Jazz Classics 1992 reissue); corrected the 'Recorded for Pablo in 1992' origin-year claim

before: The best-selling jazz album of all time, Kind of Blue introduced modal jazz to the world. Recorded at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in New York on March 2 and April 22, 1959, the album captures spontaneous creativity at its peak—most tracks were first takes with minimal rehearsal. The legendary sextet featured John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley on saxophones, pianist Bill Evans (Wynton Kelly on 'Freddie Freeloader'), bassist Paul Chambers, and drummer Jimmy Cobb. Evans contributed the album's modal framework, drawing from impressionist composers like Ravel and his studies with theorist George Russell. Revolutionized jazz by moving away from bebop's complex chord changes to modal improvisation based on scales, opening new harmonic possibilities that influenced countless musicians across genres from rock to hip-hop. It remains the quintessential introduction to jazz, selling over five million copies and consistently ranking among the greatest albums ever recorded in any genre.
after: The best-selling jazz album of all time, Kind of Blue introduced modal jazz to the world. Recorded at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in New York on March 2 and April 22, 1959, the album captures spontaneous creativity at its peak—Davis called for almost no rehearsal. The legendary sextet featured John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley on saxophones, pianist Bill Evans (Wynton Kelly on 'Freddie Freeloader'), bassist Paul Chambers, and drummer Jimmy Cobb. Evans contributed the album's modal framework, drawing from impressionist composers like Ravel and his studies with theorist George Russell. Revolutionized jazz by moving away from bebop's complex chord changes to modal improvisation based on scales, opening new harmonic possibilities that influenced countless musicians across genres from rock to hip-hop. It remains the quintessential introduction to jazz, selling over five million copies and consistently ranking among the greatest albums ever recorded in any genre.
evidenceUsed: wiki: one-pass legend 'untrue', only 'Flamenco Sketches' yielded a complete first take, but 'Davis... called for almost no rehearsal'; removed the false 'most tracks were first takes' half, kept the supported minimal-rehearsal point

before: Recorded in 2004, Latin Jazz Quintet showcases Latin Jazz Quintet at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. Latin Jazz Quintet remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Latin Jazz Quintet's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.

before: Alto saxophonist Arthur Blythe's major label debut is a thrilling collision of free jazz energy, hard bop swing, and street-level funk. His soaring, vocalized tone—one of the most distinctive in jazz—wails over James Newton's flute and Bob Stewart's tuba with ecstatic abandon. Proved that avant-garde jazz could work on a major label without compromise. Blythe's combination of free jazz fire and deep blues feeling created a uniquely accessible brand of creative music.
after: Alto saxophonist Arthur Blythe's major label debut is a thrilling collision of free jazz energy, hard bop swing, and street-level funk. His soaring, vocalized tone—one of the most distinctive in jazz—wails over James Newton's flute and Bob Stewart's tuba with ecstatic abandon. Proved that avant-garde jazz could work on a major label without compromise. Blythe's combination of free jazz fire and deep blues feeling created a uniquely accessible brand of creative music.
evidenceUsed: Flagged bad claim is the keyTrack 'Bush Baby' (absent from the MB tracklist; the album's fourth track is 'Odessa'), not the prose; the prose text contains no flagged factual error, so it is preserved verbatim and the keyTracks correction is handled in that field

before: Look What I Got! is a 1988 album by vocalist Betty Carter, released on Verve. The record won Carter a Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Female, capping a long career of uncompromising artistry. Her inventive phrasing and rhythmic daring are on full display across a program of standards and originals. Introduced Carter to a new generation after years of self-produced independence. Her uncompromising artistry finally received mainstream recognition.
after: Look What I Got! is a 1988 album by vocalist Betty Carter, released on Verve. The record won Carter a Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Female, capping a long career of uncompromising artistry. Her inventive phrasing and rhythmic daring are on full display across a program of standards and originals. Introduced Carter to a new generation after years of self-produced independence. Her uncompromising artistry finally received mainstream recognition.
evidenceUsed: Flagged bad claim is the keyTrack 'Tight' (not a standalone track; the album's track is 'Mr. Gentleman (Sequel to Tight)'), not the prose; the prose text contains no flagged factual error, so it is preserved verbatim and the keyTracks correction is handled in that field

before: Love Notes is a 1994 post-bop album by Betty Carter, recorded for the Jazz Door label. Carter, one of the most inventive vocal improvisers in jazz, applies her signature behind-the-beat phrasing and harmonic adventurousness to a program of standards and original material. He came to fame as a teen pop singer in the late 1990s, establishing himself as a star among preteen and teenage audiences during the first years of the 2000s, with his four studio albums.
after: Love Notes is a 1994 post-bop album by Betty Carter, recorded for the Jazz Door label. Carter, one of the most inventive vocal improvisers in jazz, applies her signature behind-the-beat phrasing and harmonic adventurousness to a program of standards and original material.
evidenceUsed: MB embedded release (Betty Carter, 1994, Jazz Door); deleted the trailing sentence grafted from the Aaron Carter page (wikipedia entityMatch=no, ignored), kept the two correct Betty Carter sentences verbatim

before: Flutist Nicole Mitchell's Afrofuturist masterwork imagines a world 200 years in the future through a suite of compositions that blend free jazz, electronic textures, and African rhythmic elements. Her Black Earth Ensemble creates vivid sonic landscapes that feel both ancient and futuristic. One of the defining works of Afrofuturist jazz, Mitchell's visionary composition demonstrated how speculative fiction and social critique could be channeled through improvised music with remarkable power.
after: Flutist Nicole Mitchell's Afrofuturist masterwork imagines a world 200 years in the future through a suite of compositions that blend free jazz, electronic textures, and African rhythmic elements. Her Black Earth Ensemble creates vivid sonic landscapes that feel both ancient and futuristic. One of the defining works of Afrofuturist jazz, Mitchell's visionary composition demonstrated how speculative fiction and social critique could be channeled through improvised music with remarkable power.
evidenceUsed: Flagged bad claims are the keyTracks 'Mandorla Awakening' and 'Ceremony of Infinite Projection' (absent from the MB tracklist), not the prose; the prose text contains no flagged factual error, so it is preserved verbatim and the keyTracks correction is handled in that field
